text-courier.simple_explanation: This message claims to be from Pakistan Post but uses a fake, shortened URL and demands immediate payment to prevent parcel destruction.
red_flags: Fake sender name 'PAKISTAN POST' with no official verification
Untrusted shortened link (http://pakpost-delivery.xyz)
Urgent threat to destroy the parcel
Request for immediate payment via unverified channel
safe_next_steps: Do not click the link or make any payment.
Contact the official Pakistan Post helpline or visit a local post office to verify your parcel status.
Check your official postal account or statement for any legitimate delivery updates.
Use the relevant service's official reporting channel if needed.

text-fbr.simple_explanation: This message claims a government refund but requests sensitive personal data and a payment link, which are classic signs of a financial scam.
red_flags: Requests submission of CNIC and bank card details
Uses urgency ('today') to pressure action
Claims a government refund without official verification
Directs user to an untrusted link for payment
safe_next_steps: Do not click the link or share any personal or financial information.
Verify refund eligibility only through the official FBR website or app.
Contact the Federal Board of Revenue directly using their official helpline.
Report the message to your mobile carrier and relevant authorities.

text-bank.simple_explanation: This message is a classic phishing attempt impersonating HBL to steal the user's OTP.
red_flags: Impersonation of a bank (HBL) with a false threat of account suspension.
Request for the OTP (One-Time Password), which is a critical security credential.
Urgent language demanding immediate action to create panic.
Untrusted sender claiming to be bank support without official verification.
safe_next_steps: Do not share the OTP or any personal data with anyone.
Contact HBL directly through their official website or verified helpline number.
Use the relevant service's official reporting channel if needed.
Monitor your bank account for unauthorized transactions.

image-courier.simple_explanation: This message claims a package delivery failed and demands an address update via a shortened link, which is a common scam tactic to steal personal data or charge fees.
red_flags: Untrusted shortened link (cut.ly) for address update
Urgent threat of package return
Unknown sender with generic name 'Leopards'
Request to update address outside normal channels
safe_next_steps: Do not click the link or update your address.
Contact your actual courier company using their official website or app.
Verify the sender's identity through official channels.
Report the message to your mobile carrier or local authorities.

image-mobile.simple_explanation: This message shows a marketplace buyer using a 'cousin' story to move the conversation to WhatsApp with a foreign number (+234), which is a known scam pattern.
red_flags: Marketplace buyer using a relative/cousin story
Request to continue negotiation on WhatsApp
Visible foreign phone number (+234)
Attempt to move transaction off-platform
safe_next_steps: Do not share personal details or payment info on WhatsApp.
Complete the transaction only within the official marketplace platform.
Report the message to the marketplace support team.
Block the sender if they attempt further contact.

image-traffic.simple_explanation: This message claims a traffic fine is overdue and demands immediate payment via a shortened link, which is a common pattern for financial fraud.
red_flags: Claims of overdue fine requiring immediate payment
Shortened URL (v1.mk) used to bypass link inspection
Sender number (+92 320...) is not an official government or Easypaisa contact
Urgent tone to pressure the user into acting quickly
safe_next_steps: Do not click the link or provide any payment information.
Contact your local traffic police or the official Easypaisi customer care via verified channels.
Check your official Easypaisi app or bank statement for any legitimate notifications.
Use the relevant service's official reporting channel if needed.
